method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
I can’t write the section with the required inline citations because no source documents were provided.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants, and its economic history has centered on resource claims and strategic use rather than a resident civilian economy.[1] The United States claimed Howland in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act, but the atoll’s guano deposits are now exhausted.[1] It was colonized from Hawaii in 1935, placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936, and used as a stopover for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[1] Modern economic activity is limited by the island’s protected status as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] Howland is included among the Pacific Remote Island Areas, whose surrounding U.S. exclusive economic zones are treated in regional assessments of fisheries and marine resources.[3][5] In one fisheries assessment, the Pacific Remote Island Areas—including Howland Island—accounted for about 2 percent of deployments within the broader U.S. Pacific region considered.[3]

==Time zone==
Howland Island uses UTC−12:00, designated nautical time zone Y (Yankee), and shares this offset with Baker Island within the United States Minor Outlying Islands.[2]  
Because UTC−12:00 is the last time zone before a calendar date ends globally, Howland and Baker Islands are used in the “Anywhere on Earth” convention as the places where a date remains valid the longest.[3]  
The island is uninhabited, so practical references to the last inhabited time zone often use UTC−11:00 instead, including places such as Niue and American Samoa.[3][5]  
At New Year, the day technically ends one hour later on Baker and Howland Islands than in the last inhabited places, although no permanent population is present there.[5]  
Howland’s UTC−12:00 offset also creates a 26-hour difference with the earliest parts of Kiribati, whose date-line arrangement places some of its territory among the first places to begin a new day.[1][5]

==History==
There is evidence of early Polynesian presence on Howland Island before its recorded encounters with European and American vessels.[4] The first recorded sighting was in 1822 by George Worth, captain of a Nantucket whaling ship, who named it Worth Island.[2] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship *Isabella* named the island Howland, after the ship’s lookout.[4]

The United States claimed Howland Island in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Guano extraction followed, with American and British interests mining the island’s organic nitrate deposits during the nineteenth century.[3] Captain Netcher formed the United States Guano Company and sent a vessel to exploit Howland in 1859, but the American Guano Company was already operating there, leading to a dispute that was settled in 1865 by allowing both companies to mine the island.[4] The guano-mining enterprises ended in 1878 after the deposits were depleted.[4]

In 1935, colonists from the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panala‘au Project, arrived from Hawaii to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] The settlement they founded on Howland was named Itascatown after the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter *Itasca*.[4] The island was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936 and was developed as a planned stopover for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

Howland Island became widely known because of Amelia Earhart’s attempted round-the-world flight in 1937.[2] Earhart and navigator Frederick J. Noonan disappeared near Howland on July 2, 1937, after departing Lae, Papua New Guinea, and never reached the island.[2] During World War II, Howland served as a lookout point for the United States military.[1] It was later incorporated into the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system and, in 2009, became part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[2][3]

==Earhart Light==
Earhart Light is a small lighthouse or day beacon built in 1937, when Howland Island’s landing strips were being prepared for Amelia Earhart’s planned refuelling stop during her round-the-world flight.[3] Although Earhart’s radio transmissions were heard on Howland, her aircraft never reached the island, leaving the beacon associated with the attempted rendezvous.[3] During the Japanese attack on Howland in World War II, the beacon was damaged, and a plaque honouring Earhart was shattered by machine-gun fire.[5] The station has been inactive since 1942, and the remaining structure is regarded as a day beacon rather than an operating lighthouse.[3][5] Earhart Light was rebuilt in the early 1960s, but it remains abandoned on the uninhabited island.[4]

==Image gallery==
The gallery includes a map of Howland Island and astronaut imagery showing the island from the International Space Station.[1][2]  
The ISS photograph, catalogued as ISS010-E-09287, was taken on 3 December 2004 during Expedition 10 with a Kodak DCS760C digital camera and an 800 mm lens.[2][3][4]  
In the image, Howland appears as a low coral island encircled by an active fringing reef, with white breakers marking the reef line.[2][4]  
NASA notes that high-resolution astronaut photographs of reefs and islands are used for map updates, reef-ecosystem assessment, and bathymetric analysis of surrounding ocean areas.[2][4]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge protects the island and adjacent marine habitat as part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge System.[1][4] When the refuge was established in 1974, it included the island’s land areas, waters, and submerged and emergent lands from the mean low-tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] After the 2009 creation of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, the refuge boundary was extended to 12 nautical miles, while the surrounding monument area out to 50 nautical miles is managed cooperatively by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and NOAA.[4] The refuge provides nesting and foraging habitat for seabirds and other marine wildlife, and the surrounding reef environment is part of the protected ecosystem.[1] Management is focused on conservation, biological monitoring, habitat assessment, and scientific research rather than public recreation, and federal planning documents proposed no public uses for the refuge.[5] Management issues have included invasive species impacts, while the broader monument area is noted for high marine biomass and for supporting protected species such as green sea turtles.[4] Federal refuge planning has also considered increased biological surveys, rare-seabird attraction projects, marine habitat exploration, and expanded use of the refuges as baseline sites for climate-change research.[5]
